NANOLLAMA
0102 0304 0506 07 080910 1112131415
CPU Only
LESSON 07 / 15

FEED-FORWARD
BLOCK &
RMSNORM

75 minMLP + NormalizationCPU

Every transformer block has two sub-layers: attention (Lesson 06) and a feed-forward network (FFN). The FFN is where the model stores and retrieves factual knowledge. Llama uses a special variant called SwiGLU plus RMSNorm instead of LayerNorm. We build both from scratch.

THE FEED-FORWARD NETWORK

After attention mixes information between tokens, the FFN processes each token independently. It applies the same MLP to every position — think of it as a per-token lookup in a learned knowledge store. Research has shown that factual associations ("Paris is the capital of France") are stored in FFN weights.

Standard transformers use two linear layers with a ReLU. Llama uses SwiGLU — a gated variant that consistently outperforms ReLU at the same parameter count. It has three weight matrices instead of two.

SwiGLU Feed-Forward Network — data flow
x[B, S, 64]
→
W_up · x[B, S, 256]
W_gate · x[B, S, 256]
→
SiLU(gate)
✕ up[B, S, 256]
→
W_down[B, S, 64]
→
output[B, S, 64]
hidden_dim = 4 * d_model = 256. Note: input and output shape identical!
What is SiLU?

SiLU (Sigmoid Linear Unit) = x * sigmoid(x). It's smooth, non-monotonic, and outputs non-zero for negative inputs unlike ReLU. The "gate" path controls how much of the "up" projection flows through — hence "gated". This learned gating mechanism is more expressive than a fixed activation.

swiglu_ffn.py
CORE CODE
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class SwiGLUFFN(nn.Module):
    """
    SwiGLU Feed-Forward Network as used in Llama.
    FFN(x) = W_down( SiLU(W_gate(x)) * W_up(x) )

    Note: 3 weight matrices, not 2 like standard FFN.
    hidden_dim is usually 4 * d_model (or 8/3 * d_model in some configs).
    """
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, hidden_dim: int):
        super().__init__()
        # W_up: projects input to hidden space
        self.w_up   = nn.Linear(d_model, hidden_dim, bias=False)
        # W_gate: learned gate that controls information flow
        self.w_gate = nn.Linear(d_model, hidden_dim, bias=False)
        # W_down: projects back to d_model
        self.w_down = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, d_model, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """x: (B, S, d_model) -> (B, S, d_model)"""
        # Compute gate and up projections in parallel
        gate = self.w_gate(x)          # (B, S, hidden_dim)
        up   = self.w_up(x)            # (B, S, hidden_dim)

        # SwiGLU activation: gate signal controls how much of 'up' passes
        activated = F.silu(gate) * up   # element-wise multiply (B, S, hidden_dim)

        # Project back down to d_model
        return self.w_down(activated)   # (B, S, d_model)

# Understand SiLU vs ReLU
x = torch.linspace(-4, 4, 9)
print("x:    ", x.tolist())
print("ReLU: ", F.relu(x).tolist())    # 0 for x<0, else x (hard cutoff)
print("SiLU: ", F.silu(x).round(decimals=2).tolist())
# SiLU: slightly negative for small negatives, smooth, no hard cutoff

# Test FFN
ffn = SwiGLUFFN(d_model=64, hidden_dim=256)
x   = torch.randn(4, 64, 64)
out = ffn(x)
print(out.shape)   # [4, 64, 64] -- shape preserved!

params = sum(p.numel() for p in ffn.parameters())
print(f"FFN params: {params:,}")
# w_up:64*256 + w_gate:64*256 + w_down:256*64 = 49,152

RMSNORM — SIMPLER THAN LAYERNORM

Normalization layers stabilize training by keeping activations in a sane range. Standard transformers use LayerNorm. Llama uses RMSNorm — simpler, faster, and works just as well.

LayerNorm subtracts the mean AND divides by std. RMSNorm skips the mean subtraction — it only divides by the root mean square. One fewer operation, same effect.

01
Compute RMS
Root mean square of the input vector across the feature dimension
rms = sqrt( mean(x²) + eps )
02
Normalize
Divide each element by the RMS — now the vector has unit RMS
x_norm = x / rms
03
Scale (gamma)
Learnable per-feature scale — lets model undo normalization if needed
output = gamma * x_norm
rmsnorm.py
FINAL CODE
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    """
    Root Mean Square Normalization (used in Llama instead of LayerNorm).
    Simpler: no mean subtraction, just divide by RMS and scale.
    """
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, eps: float = 1e-6):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps   = eps
        # Learnable scale parameter, initialized to ones
        self.gamma = nn.Parameter(torch.ones(d_model))

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """x: (..., d_model) -> (..., d_model). Works on any leading dims."""
        # Compute RMS along last dimension (d_model)
        # x.pow(2).mean(-1): mean of squares along last dim -> (...,)
        # keepdim=True: keep the last dim for broadcasting -> (..., 1)
        rms = x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True).add(self.eps).sqrt()

        # Normalize and scale
        return self.gamma * (x / rms)

# Test
norm = RMSNorm(64)
x    = torch.randn(4, 64, 64)   # [B, S, d_model]
out  = norm(x)
print(out.shape)   # [4, 64, 64] -- shape unchanged

# Verify normalization: after RMSNorm, rms of each vector should be ~1
rms_after = out.pow(2).mean(-1).sqrt()  # [4, 64]
print(rms_after.mean().item())  # ~1.0 (because gamma=ones initially)

# Compare with LayerNorm (for reference -- we won't use it)
layer_norm = nn.LayerNorm(64)
out_ln     = layer_norm(x)
# LayerNorm also subtracts the mean -- one more operation than RMSNorm
# Llama paper shows RMSNorm is equally effective and faster

# Why eps? Prevents division by zero when x is all zeros
zero_x = torch.zeros(1, 1, 64)
print(norm(zero_x).sum())  # 0.0 (safe, no NaN)
✓
Where is RMSNorm applied? In Llama (pre-norm architecture), normalization is applied before each sub-layer (attention and FFN), not after. This is called "pre-norm" and trains more stably than the original "post-norm" transformer. We'll wire this in Lesson 08.
⚠
eps matters. The 1e-6 epsilon prevents division by zero. In half-precision (fp16) training, use a larger eps like 1e-5 because fp16 underflows more easily. For our CPU float32 training, 1e-6 is fine.

PARAMETER COUNT CHECK

param_count.py
CHECK
# nanoLlama per-block parameter count
d_model    = 64
n_heads    = 4
hidden_dim = 256   # 4 * d_model

# MultiHeadAttention
mha_params = 3 * d_model * d_model + d_model * d_model
# qkv_proj:  3*64*64 = 12,288
# out_proj:  64*64   =  4,096
# total:             = 16,384

# SwiGLUFFN
ffn_params = 2 * d_model * hidden_dim + hidden_dim * d_model
# w_up:   64*256 = 16,384
# w_gate: 64*256 = 16,384
# w_down: 256*64 = 16,384
# total:         = 49,152

# RMSNorm (two of them per block)
norm_params = 2 * d_model   # = 128

total_per_block = mha_params + ffn_params + norm_params
print(f"Per block: {total_per_block:,}")     # 65,664

n_layers = 4
print(f"All layers: {total_per_block*n_layers:,}")  # 262,656

# Plus embedding + output head (weight tied)
embed_params = 256 * d_model   # = 16,384
print(f"Total nanoLlama: ~{(total_per_block*n_layers + embed_params):,}")
# ~279,040 parameters — tiny! Trains on CPU in minutes.

What You Now Know

  • FFN processes each token independently after attention mixes them
  • SwiGLU uses 3 weight matrices: W_up, W_gate, W_down
  • SiLU(gate) * up is the gating mechanism — more expressive than ReLU
  • RMSNorm normalizes by root mean square, not mean+std like LayerNorm
  • Pre-norm (normalize before each sub-layer) is more stable than post-norm
  • How do we wire MHA + FFN + RMSNorm into a complete block? (next lesson)
Next Lesson
LESSON 08 — THE TRANSFORMER BLOCK
